sources.name: StrategyWiki — Ghosts 'n Goblins / How to Play
sources.notes: Confirms: jump is fully committed arc (no air control), walking faster than jump traversal.

notes: Ghosts 'n Goblins is one of the most brutally difficult NES games — every design choice emphasizes player punishment.
Jump is a FULLY COMMITTED ARC — more restrictive than even Castlevania (no directional adjustment).
TWO-HIT KILL system: first hit strips Arthur's armor (leaves him in underwear), second hit kills.
Must complete the game TWICE to see the real ending.
Enemies respawn when Arthur walks off-screen.
Knockback on damage throws Arthur back (like Castlevania) but mid-jump damage is especially fatal.
Walk speed: community confirms walking is faster than using jumps for traversal — jump only when necessary.
No slide, no dash, no special mobility — pure methodical traversal against constant enemy pressure.

walk_speed.notes: Approximate from gameplay observation. Community confirms walking is faster traversal than jumping. VERIFY against ROM.

ground_movement.notes: No run, no dash. Arthur walks at constant speed.

jump._note: THE MOST RESTRICTIVE JUMP MECHANIC IN THE DOCUMENTED GAMES. Once committed, the arc is completely fixed.
notes: A jump in Ghosts 'n Goblins is 100% commitment. No direction change, no height adjustment mid-air.
This is MORE restrictive than Castlevania (which also has no air control but only has fixed height, not fixed direction).
The lack of any air control means players must plan their jump arc before pressing the button.
This makes GnG one of the games where 'thinking before jumping' is the primary skill requirement.
Community tip: 'Jump the least possible — only when necessary.' Walking is safer and faster.
Getting hit in mid-air causes knockback that continues from your current airborne position — often fatal.

gravity.notes: Gravity not explicitly documented. Arc appears similar to NES Castlevania — fast fall after peak.

damage_system._note: The two-hit system is the defining damage model of GnG — it's explicitly designed to be punishing but fair.

first_hit.effect: Strips Arthur's armor — he falls to his underwear
first_hit.armor_restored_by: Collecting armor pickup from a hidden chest
first_hit.notes: The visible 'underwear' state is both humiliating and a clear damage indicator. Arthur is completely vulnerable — aesthetically communicates the danger.

second_hit.effect: Death — Arthur turns into a pile of bones
second_hit.notes: Getting hit while in underwear = instant death. No continuing the fight.

golden_armor.description: Found in hidden chests. Enables the magical weapons.
golden_armor.notes: Golden Armor is required for the real ending — must have it during the final boss fight.

respawn_invincibility_frames.notes: Brief invincibility after being hit. VERIFY.

lance.description: Default weapon. Travels horizontally at medium speed. Medium damage.

dagger.description: Fast, short-range. Good rate of fire.

torch.description: Fires forward and slightly arcs up. Slow.

axe.description: High arc trajectory. Covers more vertical space.

magical_weapons.description: Each weapon type has a powered-up version available only while wearing the Golden Armor.

weapons.pickup_mechanic: Found in hidden chests scattered throughout stages. Collecting replaces current weapon.

notes: Knockback direction is away from the damage source.
Knockback while jumping is particularly dangerous — it alters the committed jump arc.
Getting knocked back into a pit = instant death regardless of armor status.
Knockback from certain enemies can send Arthur backward off a platform into a previously cleared area.

level_structure.enemy_respawn: Enemies respawn when Arthur scrolls off-screen and back

two_loops_required.description: After beating the final boss, the game restarts. Only by completing it TWICE is the true ending seen.
two_loops_required.notes: The second loop is identical to the first — same stages, same enemies, same difficulty.

arthur_standing.notes: Arthur hitbox is relatively generous — the game is hard enough without unfair hitboxes.

arthur_underwear.notes: Same hitbox as armored. Underwear is purely visual/damage-state indicator.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The fully-committed jump (no air control) combined with high enemy density and respawning creates a game where 'not jumping' is often the optimal strategy. Walk under enemies, walk around obstacles, jump only when there's no ground alternative.

The two-hit system creates a visible vulnerability state (underwear) that functions as both humor and danger indicator. The first hit doesn't kill — it warns. This is brilliant HCI: the game communicates threat level through Arthur's clothing.
Enemy respawning off-screen means 'progress' doesn't feel permanent — the same stretch of ground requires re-clearing every time you retreat. This creates momentum loss as a punishment mechanic.
The two-loop requirement for the real ending is divisive design: it expects players to play through the entire game twice. The modern equivalent would be NG+ required for the true ending.
The committed jump arc's real skill requirement: pattern recognition before jumping. Players who understand GnG deeply are calculating 3-4 seconds ahead of their inputs.
